title: Add orca-fork skill
description:
Create a new `orca-fork` skill that branches the current AI coding agent conversation and opens the branch in a new cmux tab.

This feature is defined by `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-fork.md` and ADR `docs/adr/0003-orca-fork-as-self-contained-skill.md`, but the critical context is repeated here so the bead is self-contained.

Domain language:
* Conversation: the provider-owned transcript/history. Codex calls this a thread; Claude Code calls this a session.
* Fork: branch an existing conversation into a new conversation and open that branch in its own tab.
* Agent instance: a live running agent type in an Orca-managed surface.
* Worker: a task-bearing agent instance. An open-only fork is an agent instance, but not a worker until it is given a task/prompt.

Why this is separate from `orca-agent`:
* `orca-agent` spawns a fresh worker from a fresh brief.
* `orca-fork` preserves prior conversation history by branching the current provider conversation.
* Forking has a different safety model because source conversation ids are provider-specific and must not be guessed.
design:
Design decisions from the grilling session:
* Build a separate `skills/orca-fork` skill, not an extension of `orca-agent`.
* Keep the skill self-contained. Runtime files inside `orca-fork` must not source, execute, or reference sibling skill scripts.
* Shared helpers that are intended to stay common, starting with `orca-cmux.sh`, should be duplicated verbatim and protected by drift tests.
* Keep fork as fire-and-confirm: open the tab, launch the fork, wait for readiness, report, and do not monitor afterward.
* Use plain terminal cmux surfaces, not native agent-session surfaces, matching existing Orca ADRs.
* Target all cmux operations by stable surface UUID, never positional refs.
* Do not persist a worker registry for forked tabs.
* Do not close a created tab after launch has started, even if readiness fails. Report the surface UUID so the human can inspect it.
* Do not expose a generic `--agent` plus `--conversation-id` interface. Conversation ids are provider-scoped and cannot be used with another agent type.
* Do not use recency fallbacks such as `codex fork --last`, `claude --continue --fork-session`, or scanning provider session files.
* Optional prompts should be passed as part of the provider launch command instead of typed after readiness.
acceptance_criteria:
Feature is complete when:
* `skills/orca-fork/SKILL.md` exists and describes the thin-wrapper workflow.
* `orca-fork` can fork Codex from `CODEX_THREAD_ID` and explicit `--codex-thread-id`.
* `orca-fork` can fork Claude from explicit `--claude-session-id`.
* Automatic Claude detection is either implemented from a verified exact source or tracked/documented as unavailable.
* The fork opens in a new cmux terminal tab in the calling workspace.
* The tab is renamed with a human-readable fork title, not a UUID.
* Fire-and-confirm output reports provider, source conversation id, surface UUID, tab name, and whether a prompt was sent.
* The package tests include `orca-fork`.
* Shared helper drift tests catch divergence between intentionally duplicated helpers.
* Existing `orca-agent` behavior and tests still pass.

title: Orca first deliverable: spawn a worker in a new tab (fire and confirm)
description:
Orca is a set of Claude Code skills and scripts that let a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers (Claude Code, Codex) in cmux tabs, hand them tasks, and observe them. See docs/prd-v1.md.

This epic is the first deliverable. The orchestrator can say "spawn a Claude or Codex worker on this task in this repo" and orca opens a new terminal tab in the CURRENT (calling) workspace, launches the agent, gets it into the right mode, hands it a file-based brief, and confirms it took. One ad-hoc task, one worker. No monitoring afterward. This is the "fire and confirm" boundary and user story 13 in the PRD.

Read before starting,
- docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md (full design)
- CONTEXT.md (glossary, especially Orchestrator, Worker, Calling workspace, Tab, Spawn, Fire and confirm)
- docs/adr/0001-plain-terminal-surfaces.md
- docs/adr/0002-target-surfaces-by-uuid.md
- docs/research/claude-mode-cycle.md and docs/research/codex-readiness.md

Build bottom-up. Child tasks,
1. cmux-interface helper script (UUID-based primitives)
2. inline agent adapters (Claude + Codex)
3. orca-spawn routine (the sequence)
4. orca-agent skill (thin conversational wrapper)
5. end-to-end manual verification

Done when a human can, by talking to the orchestrator, spawn a Claude worker that reaches "auto mode on" and a Codex worker that reaches ready, each receiving its brief, with the worker tab opening in the calling workspace and the orchestrator's own surface never disturbed.

title: cmux-interface helper script (UUID-based primitives)
description:
Part of the orca first deliverable, "spawn a worker (fire and confirm)". Orca lets a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers in cmux tabs. Full design in docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md. Read that plus CONTEXT.md and docs/adr/0001 and 0002 first.

WHAT TO BUILD
A small shell script wrapping the cmux operations orca needs, so spawn and workflow logic never call cmux directly. This is the seam a future tmux backend would replace (PRD user story 10).

Operations,
- create-tab, create a terminal surface in a given workspace and return its stable surface UUID
- send, send literal text to a surface by UUID
- send-key, send a key (enter, shift+tab, etc.) to a surface by UUID
- read-screen, read N lines from a surface by UUID
- close, close a surface by UUID
- list, list surfaces (for debugging)

VERIFIED FACTS (cmux 0.64.16, the orchestrator runs inside cmux, socket at ~/.local/state/cmux/cmux.sock, no allowAll needed)
- Create, `cmux new-surface --type terminal --workspace <ws-uuid> --focus false --id-format both` prints `OK surface:N (UUID) pane:N (UUID) workspace:N (UUID)`. The FIRST UUID in parentheses is the surface UUID. Use --focus false so spawning does not steal the human's focus.
- Send text, `cmux send --surface <uuid> "<text>"`. It does NOT auto-press enter.
- Send key, `cmux send-key --surface <uuid> enter` and `cmux send-key --surface <uuid> "shift+tab"`. The literal string shift+tab is the correct syntax, verified.
- Read, `cmux read-screen --surface <uuid> --lines 40`.
- Close, `cmux close-surface --surface <uuid>`.

CRITICAL INVARIANT (ADR 0002)
surface:N / pane:N / tab:N refs are POSITIONAL and DRIFT as surfaces open and close. They are NOT stable handles. Only UUIDs are stable. The script must accept and return UUIDs only, capture the UUID from `new-surface --id-format both`, and never store or act on a positional ref. Acting on a drifted ref already closed the orchestrator's own surface once during research.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- create-tab returns a surface UUID that round-trips, send / read-screen / close all work when targeted by that UUID.
- No operation uses a positional ref for a stored or deferred action.
- The script is independently runnable, and its command construction can be unit-checked (assert it emits the right cmux command for given inputs).

DEPENDENCIES
None. Foundation task.
close_reason: Implemented bin/orca-cmux (UUID-only cmux seam: create-tab/send/send-key/read-screen/close/list) with 22 unit tests asserting exact cmux argv via CMUX_BIN fake; shellcheck clean; live e2e round-trip verified

title: Inline agent adapters for Claude and Codex
description:
Part of the orca first deliverable, "spawn a worker (fire and confirm)". Orca lets a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers in cmux tabs. Full design in docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md. Read that plus CONTEXT.md and docs/research/claude-mode-cycle.md and codex-readiness.md first.

WHAT TO BUILD
Two adapter definitions, inline as a per-agent function or case block (extractable to a config file later, do not over-engineer now). Each adapter provides a launch command, a readiness marker, and an OPTIONAL mode step. The orca-spawn routine consumes these. An adapter is the only place that knows agent-specific details.

ADAPTER FACTS (verified on cmux 0.64.16, Claude Code v2.1.190, Codex v0.142.0)

Claude,
- launch command, `claude`
- readiness marker, the footer substring `← for agents`. It appears in EVERY mode state including default, so it reliably means the input box is up regardless of which mode Claude launched in.
- mode step, cycle until auto. While the footer does not contain `auto mode on`, send shift+tab and re-read, up to five attempts, then fail. Match on the mode NAME text, not the glyph (the glyph ⏵⏵ is shared by accept-edits and auto). Observed cycle, default (no indicator) then accept edits then plan then auto then wraps to default.

Codex,
- launch command, `codex -p yolo`. The yolo profile bakes in the approval posture, the launch banner shows "permissions: YOLO mode".
- readiness marker, the `›` input chevron. A secondary signal is the footer `<model> · <cwd>`, but model and cwd vary so prefer the chevron. Non-blocking MCP startup warnings can print above the input, so key readiness on the chevron, not on absence of warnings.
- mode step, none.

DESIGN NOTES
- Keep all marker strings in the adapter, never hardcoded in control logic, so a cmux or agent version bump that changes footer text is a one-line config edit. This is the whole reason the abstraction exists.
- The schema must support an OPTIONAL mode step, Claude uses it, Codex does not.
- Do NOT hardcode a Shift+Tab press count. Cycle until the target marker appears. The bypass-permissions mode is absent here (enterprise policy) but may exist on other machines and change the cycle length.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Both adapters are defined and selectable by agent type.
- The schema cleanly expresses "no mode step" (Codex) and "cycle to auto mode on" (Claude).
- No marker string is duplicated into the spawn logic.

DEPENDENCIES
None for definition. Consumed by orca-spawn.
close_reason: Implemented bin/orca-adapter (claude + codex adapters, selectable by type; optional mode step expressed as mode=none vs mode=cycle/auto mode on; markers defined once). 17 unit tests, marker glyph bytes verified (U+2190, U+203A), shellcheck clean

title: orca-spawn routine for launch, readiness, mode, brief, confirm
description:
Part of the orca first deliverable, "spawn a worker (fire and confirm)". Orca lets a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers in cmux tabs. Full design in docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md. Read that plus CONTEXT.md and docs/adr/0001 and 0002 first.

WHAT TO BUILD
The orca-spawn routine that performs the full spawn sequence using the cmux-interface helper and the adapters. Inputs, agent type, task title, brief text, and an optional cwd.

SEQUENCE
1. Resolve the calling workspace UUID via `cmux identify --json --id-format both`. Default the cwd to the calling workspace directory, allow an explicit override. The worker tab opens in the CALLING workspace, never a new or fixed one.
2. Generate a task id, a kebab slug from the task title. Handle collisions (append a short suffix).
3. Write the brief to <cwd>/.orca/briefs/<task-id>.md. Ensure .orca/ is listed in <cwd>/.gitignore, add it if missing.
4. create-tab in the calling workspace and capture the worker surface UUID. EVERY later operation targets this UUID (ADR 0002).
5. Launch via the adapter, send the launch command then enter.
6. Poll read-screen until the adapter readiness marker appears, with a timeout.
7. If the adapter has a mode step, run it. For Claude, cycle shift+tab until the footer contains `auto mode on`, max five attempts. For Codex, skip.
8. Deliver the brief, send the single line `Read .orca/briefs/<task-id>.md and carry out the task it describes.` then enter.
9. Return the task id, the worker surface UUID, and the tab name.

ERROR HANDLING
On any failure (readiness marker never appears within the timeout, mode never reaches `auto mode on` within five attempts, agent binary missing, cmux unreachable), report a clear error that includes the worker surface UUID and LEAVE the tab open so the human can flip to it and see what happened. Do NOT close the tab on failure, that destroys the evidence.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Spawning Claude reaches `auto mode on` and then sends the pointer brief.
- Spawning Codex reaches ready and then sends the pointer brief.
- The brief file exists at .orca/briefs/<task-id>.md and .orca/ is gitignored.
- Returns the worker surface UUID.
- A forced failure leaves the tab open and reports the UUID, and never disturbs the orchestrator's own surface.

DEPENDENCIES
Depends on the cmux-interface helper script and the inline agent adapters.
close_reason: Implemented bin/orca-spawn (resolve workspace, write brief, gitignore, create tab, launch, poll readiness, cycle mode, deliver brief, confirm). 41 integration tests over a stateful fake cmux; live e2e verified: real Claude reached 'auto mode on' and real Codex reached ready, both received the pointer brief and replied CONFIRMED. Forced failures leave the tab open and report the surface UUID. shellcheck clean. Filed orca-9gw for the untrusted-cwd trust-prompt edge.

title: orca-agent skill, thin conversational wrapper
description:
Part of the orca first deliverable, "spawn a worker (fire and confirm)". Orca lets a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers in cmux tabs. Full design in docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md. Read that plus CONTEXT.md first.

WHAT TO BUILD
A thin Claude Code skill named orca-agent that is the conversational surface for spawning a worker. The orchestrator invokes it with an agent type (claude or codex), a task (title plus brief text), and an optional cwd. The skill calls the orca-spawn routine, then reports the returned task id, worker surface UUID, and tab name back to the human.

The skill must be THIN. All real work lives in orca-spawn and the cmux-interface helper. The skill's job is to gather the agent type, the task brief, and the cwd from the conversation, shell out to orca-spawn, and relay the result. It does not reimplement any spawn, launch, mode, or read logic.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Invoking the skill with something like "spawn a codex worker on <task> in <repo>" results in a new tab in the calling workspace, a launched and ready Codex worker that received the brief, and a reported worker surface UUID.
- The same for Claude, reaching `auto mode on`.
- The skill delegates to orca-spawn and does not duplicate its logic.
- The skill reports the task id, worker surface UUID, and tab name to the human.

DEPENDENCIES
Depends on the orca-spawn routine.
close_reason: Added skills/orca-agent/SKILL.md, a thin conversational wrapper (54 lines) that gathers agent type/task/brief/cwd, shells out to orca-spawn, and relays task id + surface UUID + tab name. Delegates entirely to orca-spawn (no duplicated spawn/launch/mode/read logic). Documented interface matches orca-spawn exactly; the underlying spawn was e2e-verified for real Claude and Codex under orca-47g.3. Full conversational e2e is orca-47g.5.

title: End-to-end manual verification of spawn
description:
Part of the orca first deliverable, "spawn a worker (fire and confirm)". Orca lets a Claude Code orchestrator session spawn AI coding workers in cmux tabs. Full design in docs/specs/2026-06-24-spawn-worker.md.

WHAT TO DO
Manually verify the first deliverable end to end against real cmux and real agents. Per the PRD testing notes, skills are validated by running them, not by mechanical tests. Use a throwaway task and clean up after.

STEPS
1. From an orchestrator session, invoke orca-agent to spawn a Claude worker on a trivial task. Confirm a new tab opens in the CURRENT workspace, Claude reaches `auto mode on`, and the brief pointer line is delivered. Confirm the returned worker surface UUID matches the actual tab.
2. Repeat for Codex with `codex -p yolo`. Confirm it reaches ready (the `›` input chevron) and receives the brief.
3. Confirm the brief file exists at .orca/briefs/<task-id>.md and that .orca/ is listed in .gitignore.
4. Force a failure (for example point at a missing binary or an unreachable cmux) and confirm orca reports the worker surface UUID and leaves the tab open for inspection.
5. Confirm throughout that the orchestrator's OWN surface is never disturbed (the UUID-targeting invariant from ADR 0002 holds even as tabs open and close).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- Both agents spawn, reach ready or auto mode on, and receive their briefs.
- The failure path leaves the tab open with a clear error that includes the UUID.
- No orphaned surfaces and no wrongly-closed surfaces, the orchestrator is untouched.

DEPENDENCIES
Depends on the orca-agent skill.

title: orca-spawn: handle Claude trust-folder prompt on untrusted cwd
description: Discovered during orca-47g.3 e2e: launching claude in a directory Claude has not trusted shows a 'Is this a project you trust?' gate (1 Yes / 2 No) BEFORE the input box, so the readiness marker never appears and orca-spawn times out. It correctly leaves the tab open and reports the surface UUID. Normal orca use spawns workers in the calling workspace dir, already trusted, so this is a v1-acceptable edge. Later options: detect the trust prompt during the readiness poll and auto-confirm (send 1 then enter), or document that worker cwds must be pre-trusted. Codex -p yolo has no such gate.
close_reason:
Implemented Claude trust prompt handling in orca-spawn. The readiness loop now detects the pre-composer trust question, sends option 1, submits it, and continues polling until the normal ready marker appears. Added fake-cmux regression coverage for a Claude trust prompt before readiness and documented the behavior in the orca-agent skill.

Verification: all tests/test-*.sh suites pass.

Implementation commit: bae51185756cb4c084420c2859c6c2acba27ccfc

title: Package orca as a Claude Code plugin with marketplace
description:
Create a native Claude Code plugin package for orca, including a marketplace, following the root-native layout used by EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin.

Current state:
* Orca's canonical skills live under skills/. Today that is one skill, skills/orca-agent/, with bundled scripts under skills/orca-agent/scripts/.
* Future orca skills should be included by the plugin automatically by packaging the skills/ directory, not by making a plugin per skill.
* The current skill uses OpenSkills/Codex-compatible frontmatter with name and description.
* Each skill must remain self-contained. Do not introduce runtime references to repo-root bin/, sibling skills, or installed cache paths.

Research notes:
* Claude Code docs say plugins are self-contained directories that can contain skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, LSP servers, monitors, bin/, settings.json, and a .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest.
* Claude Code plugin skills are namespaced by plugin name, for example /my-plugin:hello.
* Claude Code marketplace distribution uses .claude-plugin/marketplace.json at the marketplace root. Users add a marketplace with /plugin marketplace add and install individual plugins with /plugin install name@marketplace.
* Claude Code copies installed plugin directories into a cache, so plugins cannot rely on paths outside the plugin directory. The existing skill-local scripts are the right packaging direction.
* Compound Engineering packages the product/plugin, not an individual skill; it uses .claude-plugin/plugin.json and .claude-plugin/marketplace.json at the repository root, with the marketplace plugin source set to ./.

Expected outcome:
* orca can be installed as a Claude Code plugin from this repository or marketplace, and the plugin includes all skills under skills/.
* The package should preserve the existing orca-agent behavior while fitting Claude Code's plugin and namespacing conventions.
design:
Proposed shape:
* Add .claude-plugin/plugin.json at repo root, modeled on Compound Engineering.
* Use plugin name orca unless a concrete platform constraint forces another stable kebab-case identifier.
* Include name, version, description, author, homepage/repository, license, and keywords for the orca plugin.
* Decide whether Claude Code can consume the existing skills/orca-agent/SKILL.md as-is or whether frontmatter needs Claude-specific adjustment. Claude examples commonly require description and support optional disable-model-invocation; Codex/OpenSkills require name and description. Preserve cross-agent compatibility where feasible.
* Keep plugin components at the plugin root level, not inside .claude-plugin/.
* Use the existing skills/ directory layout so the orca plugin includes the full skill catalog, currently skills/orca-agent/ and any future skills added under skills/.
* Add .claude-plugin/marketplace.json at repo root with marketplace name, owner, metadata, and plugins[] entry for orca, source ./, description, author, homepage, and tags.
* Add tests that validate Claude manifest JSON, marketplace JSON, source path resolution, and installed-copy assumptions: every skills/*/SKILL.md and every required executable .sh script stays inside the plugin tree.
* Validate locally with claude --plugin-dir . when available, then /reload-plugins and /orca:orca-agent or the actual namespaced skill name. Also validate /plugin marketplace add against the local path when possible.
acceptance_criteria:
* .claude-plugin/plugin.json exists, is valid JSON, and uses stable plugin identity orca with explicit versioning.
* .claude-plugin/marketplace.json exists and is valid JSON with a plugins[] entry for orca whose source points to ./, matching Compound Engineering's root-native layout.
* Claude package tests fail if any skill runtime file resolves outside the plugin tree or if any skills/*/SKILL.md is omitted from plugin packaging.
* A local smoke test demonstrates Claude Code can load the orca plugin with --plugin-dir . or via the local marketplace path, or the bead documents the exact blocker and next command to run after upgrading Claude Code.
* Install docs include Claude Code marketplace commands, including /plugin marketplace add and /plugin install orca@<marketplace>.
* No duplicate runtime scripts are introduced outside skill/plugin package content.
close_reason:
Implemented Claude Code plugin packaging for Orca.

* Added .claude-plugin/plugin.json with plugin identity orca, version 0.1.0, and skills: ./skills/.
* Added .claude-plugin/marketplace.json with marketplace namespace chrisedwards and root-native plugin source ./.
* Added README Claude Code marketplace, install, local --plugin-dir, namespaced skill, and validation instructions.
* Extended the existing package test with simple Claude manifest, marketplace, skill-path, and root-source checks.
* Kept runtime scripts inside skills/orca-agent/scripts/ with no duplicate wrappers.

Verification:
* tests/test-orca-skill-package.sh: 39 passed, 0 failed
* for test in tests/test-*.sh; do "$test" || exit $?; done: 17/23/39/58 passed, 0 failed
* python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .: passed
* claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/plugin.json --strict: passed
* claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/marketplace.json --strict: passed
* claude --plugin-dir . --print "Reply with exactly: orca plugin loaded": passed
* Isolated Claude marketplace smoke with temporary HOME added chrisedwards, installed orca@chrisedwards, and listed it enabled.

Commit: 2cc004c8256354898966f50439047c4c81506641

title: Support target workspace spawning in orca-spawn
description:
Implement target workspace support for `orca-spawn`.

Why this exists:
A future PR-review skill should be able to parse a PR link, resolve or clone the repository, then ask `orca-spawn` to start a review worker in that repository's cmux workspace. `orca-spawn` should own cmux workspace routing and worker launch mechanics, while higher-level skills own PR URL parsing, repository discovery, cloning, fetching, and review-specific brief construction.

Read before starting:
* `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-spawn-target-workspace.md`
* `docs/adr/0004-orca-spawn-owns-target-workspace-resolution.md`
* `CONTEXT.md`, especially Target workspace, Workspace name, Workspace id, Worker working directory, Current spawn context, and Workspace selection policy
* `skills/orca-spawn/SKILL.md`
* `skills/orca-spawn/scripts/orca-spawn.sh`
* `skills/orca-spawn/scripts/orca-cmux.sh`

Boundary:
* `orca-spawn` chooses the calling workspace by default.
* `orca-spawn` may resolve a target workspace by exact name or UUID.
* `orca-spawn` creates a missing named target workspace in the caller's current cmux window.
* `orca-spawn` creates the worker surface in the target workspace, launches the agent, waits for readiness, delivers the brief, and reports key values.
* `orca-spawn` never parses PR URLs, clones repos, creates working directories, fetches code, or constructs review-specific briefs.
* `orca-fork` remains calling-workspace-only for this feature.
design:
Decisions from the grilling session:
* Add `--workspace-name <name>` for human-readable target workspace selection. Match exact workspace `custom_title` or `title` inside the caller's current cmux window. If exactly one match exists, use it. If zero matches exist, create it. If multiple exact matches exist, fail as ambiguous and tell the caller to use `--workspace-id`.
* Add `--workspace-id <uuid>` for machine-stable target workspace selection. It must be a real UUID, must exist in the caller's current cmux window, and never creates a workspace. Reject positional refs such as `workspace:3`.
* If no workspace selector is supplied, preserve current behavior: target the calling workspace.
* `--cwd` is the worker working directory and can differ from the target workspace's `current_directory`.
* If `--cwd` is omitted, default from the selected existing target workspace's `current_directory`. If creating a named workspace and no `--cwd` is supplied, default from the caller's current workspace `current_directory`.
* Validate the resolved cwd exists before creating a workspace or worker surface. `orca-spawn` must not create directories or clone repositories.
* Created workspaces use the supplied/resolved cwd as their initial directory and are created with no focus stealing.
* Always create a separate worker surface inside the target workspace; do not reuse the initial terminal surface created by `cmux new-workspace`.
* Output should always include `workspace=<uuid>`, `workspace_name=<name-if-known>`, and `workspace_created=true|false` in addition to existing fields.
acceptance_criteria:
* `orca-spawn` can spawn into the calling workspace with no workspace selector, preserving existing behavior.
* `orca-spawn` can spawn into an existing workspace selected by exact `--workspace-name` in the caller's current cmux window.
* `orca-spawn` creates a missing named workspace in the caller's current cmux window and then spawns the worker there.
* `orca-spawn` can spawn into an existing workspace selected by UUID with `--workspace-id`.
* Duplicate exact workspace-name matches fail as ambiguous without creating a workspace or worker surface.
* Unknown or positional workspace ids fail before creating a workspace or worker surface.
* Explicit `--cwd` is honored as the worker working directory even when it differs from target workspace metadata.
* Omitted `--cwd` uses the selected target workspace `current_directory`, or the caller current workspace `current_directory` when creating a missing named workspace.
* Missing resolved cwd fails before creating any workspace or worker surface.
* Workspace creation and worker surface creation do not steal focus.
* Success output includes `workspace`, `workspace_name`, `workspace_created`, `surface`, `tab`, `cwd`, and `brief`.
* The `orca-spawn` skill wrapper documents and supports target workspace inputs.
* Full shell test suite and plugin packaging validators pass.

title: Add reply footer to spawn and fork messages
description:
Add the same reply-routing footer used by `orca-msg` to messages created by `orca-agent` and `orca-fork`, so spawned workers and forked conversations know which parent surface to reply to.

Expected behavior:
* `orca-agent` appends a footer to the brief delivery message identifying the caller/origin surface and instructing the worker to use `orca-msg` to reply if needed.
* `orca-fork` appends the footer to any prompt passed at fork launch.
* Open-only forks with no prompt should remain open-only and not send a new turn just to deliver a footer.
* If the origin surface cannot be resolved, use a fallback footer explaining that the origin surface was unavailable.
* Keep all skill runtime files self-contained.
acceptance_criteria:
* Fake-cmux spawn tests assert the brief delivery includes the origin surface footer.
* Fake-cmux fork tests assert prompted forks include the origin surface footer in the launch command.
* Open-only fork behavior remains unchanged.
* Shared `orca-cmux.sh` helper parity remains green.
* Full `tests/test-*.sh` suite and plugin validators pass.
close_reason:
Added parent reply-routing footers to spawn and prompted fork messages.

* `orca-agent` now appends a parent footer to the brief pointer message sent to spawned workers.
* `orca-fork` now appends the same parent footer to prompts passed at fork launch.
* Open-only forks remain open-only and do not send a footer-only prompt.
* Added `Parent` to `CONTEXT.md` as the agent instance that spawned or forked another instance and is the reply target for that relationship.
* Updated fake-cmux spawn and fork tests to assert the parent surface footer.

Verification:
* `tests/test-orca-spawn.sh`
* `tests/test-orca-fork.sh`
* `for test in tests/test-*.sh; do "$test" || exit $?; done`
* `python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/plugin.json --strict`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/marketplace.json --strict`
* `git diff --check`

Implementation commit: cf737ea

title: Implement orca-fork fire-and-confirm MVP
description:
Implement the first end-to-end `orca-fork` skill from the design captured in `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-fork.md`.

The MVP must create a new forked conversation in a cmux tab using exact provider-specific conversation ids only. It should support Codex automatic source discovery through `CODEX_THREAD_ID`, explicit Codex source through `--codex-thread-id <uuid>`, and explicit Claude source through `--claude-session-id <uuid>`. It must not support a generic `--agent` or generic `--conversation-id` because conversation ids are provider-scoped.

This is fire-and-confirm only. It launches the fork, waits for readiness, reports key/value output, and stops. It does not monitor the fork afterward and does not persist a worker registry.
design:
Skill and files:
* Add `skills/orca-fork/SKILL.md` as a thin conversational wrapper around bundled scripts.
* Add `skills/orca-fork/scripts/orca-fork.sh` for orchestration.
* Add `skills/orca-fork/scripts/orca-cmux.sh` as an exact duplicate of the shared cmux seam when intended to stay in sync with `orca-agent`.
* Add `skills/orca-fork/scripts/orca-fork-adapter.sh` or equivalent fork-specific adapter config for provider source detection, command construction, and readiness markers.
* Do not call or source files from `skills/orca-agent` or any other sibling skill.

Source selection:
* Auto Codex source: `CODEX_THREAD_ID`.
* Explicit Codex source: `--codex-thread-id <uuid>`.
* Explicit Claude source: `--claude-session-id <uuid>`.
* If exactly one auto source exists and no explicit source is passed, use it.
* If one explicit provider-specific source is passed, use it and ignore auto sources.
* If multiple explicit provider-specific sources are passed, fail.
* If multiple auto sources are available and no explicit source is passed, fail as ambiguous.
* If no exact source is available, fail with an actionable message.
* Do not use `codex fork --last`, `claude --continue --fork-session`, or provider session-file scanning.

Launch commands:
* Codex command shape: `codex fork <thread-id> [prompt]`.
* Claude command shape: `claude --resume <session-id> --fork-session [prompt]`.
* Pass an optional prompt as part of the launch command rather than typing it after the TUI is ready.

cmux behavior:
* Resolve the calling workspace with `cmux identify --json --id-format both`.
* Resolve cwd from the calling workspace unless a safe cwd override is intentionally added.
* Create a plain terminal surface in the calling workspace.
* Use `--focus false` for tab creation.
* Parse and store the created surface UUID from `cmux new-surface --id-format both`.
* Target later send/read/rename operations by UUID only.
* Rename the tab after creation.
* Wait for provider readiness using adapter readiness markers.
* Leave any created tab open on post-launch failure and include `surface=<uuid>` in output when known.

Tab naming:
* Open-only fork default: `fork-codex` or `fork-claude`.
* Prompted fork: derive a short slug from the prompt or provided title, such as `fork-investigate-login`.
* Support `--title <title>` override.
* Return the requested tab name in `tab=<name>`.
* Do not put conversation UUIDs in the tab title.

Output contract:
* On success print `status=ok`, `provider=codex|claude`, `source_conversation=<provider-specific-id>`, `surface=<surface-uuid>`, `tab=<tab-name>`, and `prompt_sent=true|false`.
* On failure print `status=error`, include values already known, include `error=<reason>`, and leave any created tab open.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
* `skills/orca-fork/SKILL.md` exists and points users at the bundled fork script.
* Runtime scripts are executable and pass `bash -n`.
* `orca-fork` supports Codex via `CODEX_THREAD_ID`.
* `orca-fork` supports explicit `--codex-thread-id <uuid>`.
* `orca-fork` supports explicit `--claude-session-id <uuid>`.
* `orca-fork` rejects multiple explicit provider-specific ids.
* `orca-fork` rejects ambiguous auto sources.
* `orca-fork` rejects missing exact source ids with an actionable error.
* `orca-fork` does not expose `--agent` or generic `--conversation-id`.
* `orca-fork` does not use recency fallback commands or provider session-file scanning.
* Prompted forks include the prompt in the launch command.
* Open-only forks launch without an initial prompt.
* Created tabs use plain terminal surfaces in the calling workspace.
* All cmux targets after creation use UUIDs only.
* Tab rename is exercised and reported.
* Success output includes provider, source conversation, surface, tab, and prompt_sent.
* Failed readiness leaves the tab open and reports the surface UUID.
* Package tests include `orca-fork`.
* Shared helper drift tests compare the duplicated `orca-cmux.sh` copies with `cmp -s` or equivalent.
* Adapter/source-selection tests cover Codex env, explicit Codex, explicit Claude, ambiguity, and no-source failures.
* Integration tests use fake cmux screens to cover prompt-at-launch, open-only readiness, tab rename, no recency fallback, and post-launch failure behavior.
* Existing `tests/test-orca-agent*`, `tests/test-orca-cmux.sh`, and `tests/test-orca-spawn.sh` behavior remains green or is intentionally updated with equivalent coverage.
notes:
Implementation note from live Codex smoke test:
* `codex fork <thread-id>` can stop at Codex's "Do you trust the contents of this directory?" prompt before the composer appears.
* The MVP handles that pre-readiness prompt by selecting the "Yes" option for the calling workspace, then continues polling for the normal Codex readiness marker.
* This behavior is covered by `tests/test-orca-fork.sh` and documented in `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-fork.md` plus `skills/orca-fork/SKILL.md`.
close_reason:
Implemented the orca-fork fire-and-confirm MVP.

* Added the self-contained `skills/orca-fork` skill with bundled fork orchestration, provider adapter, and verbatim cmux seam copy.
* Supports Codex auto source via `CODEX_THREAD_ID`, explicit `--codex-thread-id`, and explicit `--claude-session-id` without generic `--agent` or `--conversation-id`.
* Launches `codex fork <thread-id> [prompt]` or `claude --resume <session-id> --fork-session [prompt]` in a new cmux terminal tab, renames the tab, waits for readiness, and reports key/value output.
* Handles Codex's trust-directory prompt discovered during live smoke testing.
* Added package, adapter, and fake-cmux integration tests, including shared-helper drift coverage.
* Updated README and plugin metadata to advertise both spawn and fork skills.

Verification:
* `for test in tests/test-*.sh; do "$test" || exit $?; done`
* `python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/plugin.json --strict`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/marketplace.json --strict`
* Live Codex smoke: `skills/orca-fork/scripts/orca-fork.sh --title fork-smoke-test` returned `status=ok`; the smoke tab was closed afterward.

Commit: 9500883a7c3e5e2b6f37a495f0d1f7a3951e8e94

title: Avoid blank-line reply footers
description:
Normalize text sent through Orca's cmux command-line seam so payloads never contain consecutive newline characters. cmux treats newlines in `send` payloads as Enter events, and consecutive newlines can make an agent UI submit the body and footer as separate messages.

Scope:
* `orca-cmux send` should collapse runs of two or more newline characters to one newline before invoking `cmux send`.
* Apply this to all copied `orca-cmux.sh` helpers.
* Preserve `send-key` behavior exactly.
* Keep footer helpers using one newline before the routing line.
acceptance_criteria:
* `tests/test-orca-cmux.sh` covers `send` newline normalization and proves `send-key` is unchanged.
* `tests/test-orca-msg.sh`, `tests/test-orca-spawn.sh`, and `tests/test-orca-fork.sh` still pass.
* Full `tests/test-*.sh` suite passes.
* Plugin validators pass.
close_reason:
Completed in e6bf022.

What changed:
* Tested the cmux hypothesis with a temporary terminal recorder: sending `alpha\n\nbeta` through `cmux send` delivered consecutive newline bytes, so blank lines can be interpreted by agent UIs as extra Enter submissions.
* Added seam-level normalization to each copied `orca-cmux.sh`: `send` collapses runs of consecutive newlines to one newline before invoking cmux.
* Kept `send-key` behavior unchanged.
* Updated orca-msg, orca-agent, and orca-fork footers to append routing context with one newline and trim trailing message newlines.

Verified:
* Full `tests/test-*.sh` suite passed.
* `python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .` passed.
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/plugin.json --strict` passed.
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/marketplace.json --strict` passed.
* `git diff --check` passed.

title: orca-spawn: expose target workspace routing in the skill
description:
Expose target workspace routing in the `orca-spawn` skill wrapper and finish validation/documentation for the feature.

Why this exists:
The script behavior is only half of the user-facing feature. Humans should be able to ask the `orca-spawn` skill to spawn a worker in a named workspace, and future higher-level skills need clear instructions for what belongs in `orca-spawn` versus their own PR/repo orchestration layer.

Read before starting:
* `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-spawn-target-workspace.md`
* `docs/adr/0004-orca-spawn-owns-target-workspace-resolution.md`
* `CONTEXT.md` target workspace terms
* All implementation beads under the target workspace parent

Scope for this slice:
* Update `skills/orca-spawn/SKILL.md` to gather optional workspace name or workspace id inputs.
* Document that the wrapper may pass `--workspace-name`, `--workspace-id`, and `--cwd` to the bundled script.
* Document the boundary: `orca-spawn` does not parse PR URLs, clone repos, fetch branches, or construct review-specific briefs.
* Update package tests or docs checks as needed so the new public options remain discoverable.
* Run full automated validation and document manual smoke-test expectations.
design:
Implementation notes:
* Keep the skill a thin conversational wrapper. It should gather inputs, construct a self-contained brief, call `scripts/orca-spawn.sh`, and relay key output values.
* If a human asks the raw `orca-spawn` skill to review a PR link without providing a local repo path, do not silently invent repo discovery. Ask for concrete cwd/workspace inputs or explain that PR URL resolution belongs to a higher-level review skill.
* Include `workspace`, `workspace_name`, `workspace_created`, `surface`, `tab`, `cwd`, and `brief` in the success report back to the human when available.
acceptance_criteria:
* `skills/orca-spawn/SKILL.md` lists optional target workspace inputs and explains when to pass `--workspace-name` or `--workspace-id`.
* The skill docs preserve the boundary that PR URL parsing, repo discovery, cloning, fetching, and review-specific brief construction are out of scope for `orca-spawn`.
* The skill's report-back guidance includes target workspace fields in addition to worker surface and tab.
* README or package docs are updated if they enumerate `orca-spawn` capabilities.
* Full `tests/test-*.sh` suite passes.
* Codex plugin validation passes.
* Claude plugin manifest and marketplace validation pass.
* Manual smoke notes cover calling workspace spawn, existing named workspace spawn, missing named workspace creation, and explicit cwd differing from target workspace metadata.

title: Implement orca-msg skill
description:
Create a new self-contained `orca-msg` skill that delivers follow-up messages to existing Claude Code or Codex agent instances in cmux terminal surfaces.

Design source: `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-msg.md`.

Key behavior:
* Accept a pasted cmux copy-ids block and extract only `surface_id` for targeting.
* Accept human surface descriptors such as "the Claude agent in the aiml-services workspace" and resolve them through cmux metadata in the caller's current cmux window.
* Ask for clarification, with candidates, when descriptor resolution finds zero or multiple plausible surfaces.
* Use the surface UUID as the only durable target; never target by positional refs.
* Detect target agent type from descriptor hints first, then screen markers, then user clarification.
* Send only when the target appears to be at a ready agent input prompt.
* Never answer permission prompts, trust prompts, shell prompts, merge editors, running-command states, or unknown blocked UI.
* Support direct short messages and file-pointer delivery for long/context-heavy messages using a temp file outside the repo.
* Report the exact message sent and any temp message file path.

Packaging:
* Add `skills/orca-msg/SKILL.md`.
* Add bundled scripts under `skills/orca-msg/scripts/`, including `orca-msg.sh`, `orca-cmux.sh`, and any readiness/adapter helper needed.
* Keep the skill self-contained; do not reference sibling skills or repo-root runtime scripts.
* Add the local development symlink under `.agents/skills/`.
* Update package/plugin tests and docs so `orca-msg` ships with `orca-agent` and `orca-fork`.
acceptance_criteria:
* `tests/test-orca-skill-package.sh` validates `orca-msg` files, executability, and shared helper parity where intended.
* Fake-cmux tests cover copy-ids parsing, UUID-only targeting, descriptor success, descriptor ambiguity without send, Claude/Codex readiness success, blocked-state refusal, direct message argument preservation, and file-pointer delivery.
* Plugin validation passes for Codex and Claude manifests.
* README names `orca-msg` alongside `orca-agent` and `orca-fork`.
close_reason:
Implemented `orca-msg` as a self-contained skill.

* Added `skills/orca-msg/SKILL.md` and bundled `scripts/orca-msg.sh` plus copied cmux seam.
* Supports stable surface UUIDs, pasted cmux copy-ids blocks, and conservative descriptor resolution in the caller's current cmux window.
* Verifies Claude/Codex readiness before sending and refuses blocked prompts or unknown UI states.
* Supports direct messages and message-file pointer delivery with absolute paths.
* Updated package docs, plugin metadata, local skill symlink, and tests.

Verification:
* `for test in tests/test-*.sh; do "$test" || exit $?; done`
* `python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/plugin.json --strict`
* `claude plugin validate .claude-plugin/marketplace.json --strict`
* `git diff --check`

Implementation commit: 2a89f59

title: orca-spawn: support workspace UUID targeting
description:
Add machine-stable target workspace selection to `orca-spawn` with `--workspace-id <uuid>`.

Why this exists:
Higher-level automation may already have a durable cmux workspace UUID. It should be able to target that exact workspace without relying on human-readable names, while still preserving Orca's invariant that positional refs are unsafe for deferred operations.

Read before starting:
* `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-spawn-target-workspace.md`
* `docs/adr/0002-target-surfaces-by-uuid.md`
* `docs/adr/0004-orca-spawn-owns-target-workspace-resolution.md`
* `CONTEXT.md` terms: Workspace id, Target workspace, Worker working directory
* The bead that adds target workspace resolution for existing named workspaces

Scope for this slice:
* Add `--workspace-id <uuid>` parsing to `orca-spawn`.
* Reject positional refs like `workspace:3`, bare indexes, and non-UUID values.
* Resolve the UUID only among workspaces in the caller's current cmux window.
* Use the selected workspace as the target workspace for worker surface creation.
* If `--cwd` is omitted, use the selected workspace's `current_directory`; if supplied, use the explicit cwd.
* Add output fields for UUID-selected workspaces.
design:
Implementation notes:
* `--workspace-name` and `--workspace-id` are mutually exclusive.
* `--workspace-id` never creates a workspace. Unknown UUIDs fail before creating a worker surface.
* Keep lookup current-window scoped for v1. Do not target workspaces in other cmux windows even if a UUID exists there.
* Reuse existing UUID validation style from `orca-cmux.sh` where possible.
acceptance_criteria:
* `orca-spawn --workspace-id <uuid>` creates the worker surface in that workspace when the UUID exists in the caller's current cmux window.
* Unknown workspace UUID fails before worker surface creation.
* Positional refs such as `workspace:3` are rejected before any cmux mutation.
* Supplying both `--workspace-name` and `--workspace-id` fails before any cmux mutation.
* Omitted `--cwd` uses the selected workspace `current_directory`.
* Explicit `--cwd` is honored as the worker working directory.
* Success output includes `workspace=<uuid>`, `workspace_created=false`, and `workspace_name` when known from cmux metadata.
* Tests prove current-window scoping and the command sequence uses UUIDs only for durable targets.

title: Verify Claude auto source detection for orca-fork
description:
Verify whether Claude Code exposes a reliable exact current session id to skill/runtime scripts, then add automatic Claude source detection to `orca-fork` only if that source is deterministic.

This is a follow-up because the design session verified that Claude Code 2.1.191 supports `--fork-session` with `--resume` or `--continue`, and verified that Claude statusline input can include `session_id`, but did not prove that an arbitrary skill script receives a reliable current-session environment variable.

The safety rule is strict: `orca-fork` must not guess the current Claude session from recency, `--continue`, `--fork-session` alone, or files under `~/.claude/projects`.
design:
Investigation constraints:
* Start from Claude Code help for the installed version and any local documented behavior.
* Determine whether a current Claude session id is available to skill/runtime scripts as an environment variable or another exact process-local source.
* Statusline `session_id` is known context, but do not assume it is available to skill scripts without proof.
* Do not parse latest JSONL files, sessions-index files, modification times, or other recency-derived sources as an automatic source.
* Do not use `claude --continue --fork-session` as automatic detection because it can pick the wrong conversation in multi-tab/multi-agent workflows.

Implementation rules if a reliable source exists:
* Add automatic Claude source detection to `orca-fork` using that exact source.
* Keep explicit `--claude-session-id <uuid>` working.
* Preserve ambiguity handling when both Codex and Claude auto sources are present.
* Add tests for the exact source.

Implementation rules if no reliable source exists:
* Keep `--claude-session-id <uuid>` as the only Claude path.
* Improve the no-source error message to explain how to provide `--claude-session-id`.
* Document the limitation in `skills/orca-fork/SKILL.md` and, if useful, a small research note.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
* The verified Claude current-session source is documented, or the absence of such a source is documented.
* No recency fallback or session-file scanning is introduced.
* If automatic Claude detection is implemented, tests prove it uses an exact source and constructs `claude --resume <session-id> --fork-session [prompt]`.
* If automatic Claude detection is not implemented, tests prove explicit `--claude-session-id` remains the only Claude path and the error message is actionable.
* Ambiguity behavior with Codex auto source plus Claude auto source, if possible, remains covered.
* The implementation remains self-contained inside `skills/orca-fork`.
close_reason: Verified CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID as an exact, process-local current-session source (Claude Code 2.1.191): it is Claude Code's own var (not cmux-injected), maps to the live transcript, and tracks /branch. Added Claude auto-detect to orca-fork.sh mirroring the Codex CODEX_THREAD_ID path, with both-present ambiguity handling and explicit-flag override preserved. No recency/file-scan fallback introduced. Documented the source (undocumented-but-verified) and the subagent caveat in SKILL.md. Tests cover claude auto source, explicit-wins-over-env, and both-auto ambiguity; harness now pins both auto vars to prevent runner env leak. All 6 suites green. Commit b73de49.

title: orca-spawn: create missing named target workspaces
description:
Extend `orca-spawn --workspace-name` so a missing named workspace is created in the caller's current cmux window before the worker is spawned.

Why this exists:
The future PR-review flow should be able to route a worker to a repository workspace even when that workspace is not already open. Once a higher-level skill has resolved or cloned the local repository path, `orca-spawn` should handle the cmux workspace creation and worker launch mechanics.

Read before starting:
* `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-spawn-target-workspace.md`
* `docs/adr/0004-orca-spawn-owns-target-workspace-resolution.md`
* `CONTEXT.md` terms: Target workspace, Workspace name, Worker working directory, Workspace selection policy
* The bead that adds existing named workspace targeting

Scope for this slice:
* When `--workspace-name <name>` has zero exact matches in the caller's current cmux window, create a cmux workspace named `<name>` in that window.
* Use explicit `--cwd` as the new workspace initial directory when supplied.
* If `--cwd` is omitted, use the caller's current workspace `current_directory` as the new workspace initial directory and worker working directory.
* Create a separate worker surface inside the newly created workspace; do not reuse the terminal surface created by `cmux new-workspace`.
* Do not steal focus when creating the workspace or worker surface.

Out of scope for this slice:
* Creating directories or cloning repositories.
* Searching outside the caller's current cmux window.
* `orca-fork` target workspace routing.
design:
Implementation notes:
* Add a cmux seam command for workspace creation, likely wrapping `cmux new-workspace --name <name> --cwd <cwd> --window <caller-window> --focus false --id-format both` or the current equivalent.
* Parse and return the created workspace UUID. Durable follow-up operations must use the UUID, not a positional ref.
* Validate the resolved cwd exists before creating the workspace.
* If workspace creation succeeds but later worker launch fails, leave created UI state open for inspection and include known `workspace` and `surface` fields in error output when available.
* If the shared `orca-cmux.sh` helper changes, update intentionally copied helper files and drift tests as needed.
acceptance_criteria:
* `orca-spawn --workspace-name <missing-name> --cwd <existing-dir>` creates a workspace named `<missing-name>` in the caller's current cmux window and spawns the worker there.
* Missing named workspace with omitted `--cwd` creates the workspace using the caller current workspace `current_directory` when that directory exists.
* Missing resolved cwd fails before workspace creation.
* Workspace creation uses no-focus behavior.
* Worker surface creation still uses no-focus behavior and targets the created workspace UUID.
* The initial terminal surface from `new-workspace` is not treated as the worker surface; `surface=<uuid>` reports the worker surface created by Orca.
* Success output includes `workspace_created=true`, the created workspace UUID, and the worker surface UUID.
* Fake-cmux tests prove the create-workspace command, create-tab command, and no-focus arguments.

title: Package orca as a Codex plugin with marketplace
description:
Create a native Codex plugin package for orca, following the root-native layout used by EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin.

Current state:
* Orca's canonical skills live under skills/. Today that is one skill, skills/orca-agent/, with bundled scripts under skills/orca-agent/scripts/.
* Future orca skills should be included by the plugin automatically by packaging the skills/ directory, not by making a plugin per skill.
* Repo-scoped Codex discovery currently uses .agents/skills/orca-agent as a symlink to ../../skills/orca-agent for local authoring.
* Each skill must remain self-contained. Do not introduce runtime references to repo-root bin/, sibling skills, or installed cache paths.

Research notes:
* Codex docs say skills are the reusable workflow format and plugins are the installable distribution unit for reusable skills and apps.
* Codex plugin authoring uses a .codex-plugin/plugin.json manifest. A plugin that ships skills can point skills at ./skills/.
* Codex repo marketplaces use .agents/plugins/marketplace.json. Compound Engineering uses a root-native plugin manifest at .codex-plugin/plugin.json and a root marketplace at .agents/plugins/marketplace.json pointing at the repo as a URL source.
* Compound Engineering packages the product/plugin, not an individual skill; its manifest points at ./skills/ so every bundled skill is part of the plugin.

Expected outcome:
* orca can be installed as a Codex plugin from this repository, and the plugin includes all skills under skills/.
* The plugin package and marketplace should be idiomatic for Codex CLI/App testing and future distribution.
design:
Proposed shape:
* Add .codex-plugin/plugin.json at repo root, modeled on Compound Engineering.
* Use plugin name orca unless a concrete platform constraint forces another stable kebab-case identifier.
* Include skills: ./skills/ so Codex packages the entire orca skill catalog, currently skills/orca-agent/ and any future skills added under skills/.
* Add interface metadata if useful: displayName Orca, shortDescription, longDescription, developerName, category Coding/Productivity, capabilities Read/Write/Interactive, websiteURL/repository, brandColor, defaultPrompt examples.
* Add .agents/plugins/marketplace.json so Codex can register the repository as a marketplace. Follow Compound Engineering's root-native style with a URL source for GitHub distribution, or use local source.path for local test fixtures if needed.
* Decide whether .agents/skills/orca-agent remains as a local-authoring convenience after the plugin exists. If kept, document that it is for repo-local authoring, while plugin install is the distribution path.
* Keep runtime files inside their owning skill directory, such as skills/orca-agent/scripts/. Do not reintroduce root compatibility wrappers or shared runtime paths outside packaged plugin content.
* Add tests that validate JSON manifests, that every manifest path resolves, and that a plugin copy/install would include every skills/*/SKILL.md plus executable bundled .sh scripts.
* Validate with codex plugin marketplace/add/install commands when available. Document exact test commands in README or docs.
acceptance_criteria:
* .codex-plugin/plugin.json exists, is valid JSON, uses plugin identity orca, and points at ./skills/.
* .agents/plugins/marketplace.json exists, is valid JSON, and exposes the orca plugin in a way Codex can register from the repo or GitHub.
* Package tests fail if the plugin manifest points outside the distributable tree, omits any skills/*/SKILL.md, or misses required skill-local scripts.
* A local smoke test demonstrates Codex can see/install the orca plugin through the marketplace path, or the bead documents the exact blocker and next command to run after upgrading Codex.
* README or install docs include Codex CLI/App install instructions matching the marketplace layout.
* No duplicate runtime scripts are introduced outside skill/plugin package content.
close_reason:
Implemented Codex plugin packaging for Orca.

* Added root .codex-plugin/plugin.json with plugin identity orca and skills: ./skills/.
* Added .agents/plugins/marketplace.json exposing orca from https://github.com/ChrisEdwards/orca.git.
* Added README Codex CLI/App install instructions, validation commands, and the observed non-interactive GitHub-auth smoke-test blocker.
* Extended the existing package test with simple manifest, marketplace, skill-path, and no-duplicate-runtime-script checks.
* Updated AGENTS.md to clarify that TDD applies when useful and should not force brittle tests for packaging metadata or external smoke checks.

Verification:
* tests/test-orca-skill-package.sh: 26 passed, 0 failed
* python3 /Users/chrisedwards/.codex/skills/.system/plugin-creator/scripts/validate_plugin.py .: passed
* for test in tests/test-*.sh; do "$test" || exit $?; done: 17/23/26/58 passed, 0 failed
* Isolated Codex smoke with temporary HOME registered the repo marketplace and listed orca@orca. Install then failed cloning https://github.com/ChrisEdwards/orca.git with: fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com': terminal prompts disabled. Next command after GitHub access is available: codex plugin add orca@orca --json.

Commit: 18ba641e63d6743eede950884f6c5ce5e4ebc0c3

title: Record orca-spawn skill rename
description:
Track the public skill rename from orca-agent to orca-spawn after the code change was completed.

The rename moved the canonical skill directory and repo-local discovery symlinks, updated skill frontmatter, docs, specs, and package tests, and left historical closed bead text unchanged.
close_reason:
Completed as a historical tracking bead for the public skill rename.

Renamed `orca-agent` to `orca-spawn` across the canonical skill directory, skill frontmatter, repo-local Codex and Claude symlinks, docs, specs, and package tests. Historical closed bead text was intentionally left unchanged.

Implementation commit: 07118cf

title: orca-spawn: target an existing workspace by exact name
description:
Add the first vertical slice of target workspace support: an `orca-spawn` call can target an existing workspace by exact human-readable name in the caller's current cmux window.

Why this exists:
Humans and future higher-level skills need to say "spawn this worker in the aiml-services workspace" without manually copying workspace UUIDs. This is the foundation for PR-review orchestration where the review worker should appear in the repository's workspace.

Read before starting:
* `docs/specs/2026-06-25-orca-spawn-target-workspace.md`
* `docs/adr/0004-orca-spawn-owns-target-workspace-resolution.md`
* `CONTEXT.md` terms: Target workspace, Workspace name, Worker working directory, Current spawn context
* Existing tests in `tests/test-orca-spawn.sh` and `tests/test-orca-cmux.sh`

Scope for this slice:
* Add `--workspace-name <name>` parsing to `orca-spawn`.
* Resolve exact matches against workspace `custom_title` and `title` in the caller's current cmux window.
* Use the matched workspace as the target workspace for worker surface creation.
* If `--cwd` is omitted, use the matched workspace's `current_directory` as the worker working directory.
* If `--cwd` is supplied, use it as the worker working directory even if it differs from workspace metadata.
* Add output fields for the existing named workspace path: `workspace`, `workspace_name`, and `workspace_created=false`.

Out of scope for this slice:
* Creating missing workspaces.
* `--workspace-id` UUID targeting.
* PR URL parsing, cloning, fetching, or review brief construction.
design:
Implementation notes:
* Keep lookup scoped to the caller's current cmux window. Do not search all windows.
* Exact name means exact string equality against `custom_title` or `title`; do not substring or fuzzy match.
* If more than one workspace exactly matches the requested name, fail as ambiguous and tell the caller to use `--workspace-id`.
* Reuse the existing UUID-only cmux boundary. The resolved target workspace is passed internally as a UUID to `create-tab`.
* Preserve existing no-selector behavior as a regression target.
acceptance_criteria:
* `orca-spawn --workspace-name <existing-name>` creates the worker surface in the matched workspace UUID, not the calling workspace UUID.
* Exact matching checks `custom_title` and `title` in the caller's current cmux window.
* Duplicate exact matches fail before creating a worker surface and include an actionable ambiguity error.
* With existing named workspace and no `--cwd`, the brief is written under that workspace's `current_directory` and the worker starts there.
* With existing named workspace and explicit `--cwd`, the brief is written under the explicit cwd and the worker starts there.
* Success output includes `workspace=<matched-workspace-uuid>`, `workspace_name=<matched-name>`, and `workspace_created=false`.
* Existing spawn behavior with no workspace selector remains green.
* Tests cover the fake-cmux command sequence and output contract.
